text.title: Prompt Engineering — Security and Injection Defense — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the prompt-engineering-security methodology. Sources: Greshake et al. — Prompt-injection survey (arXiv:2302.12173); OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications; Anthropic constitutional AI + injection defences.

rule.statement: Wrap all user-supplied content in clearly-delimited blocks (XML tags `<user_input>...</user_input>`); never concatenate untrusted text into instruction prose.
rule.rationale: Delimited blocks tell the model what is data vs instruction; concatenation is the canonical injection vector.

rule.statement: Use the system → developer → user instruction hierarchy: hard rules in system, soft preferences in developer, content in user; never put hard rules in user-supplied text.
rule.rationale: Models respect the hierarchy more than the text; sensitive rules survive injection attempts only when they live in the right tier.

rule.statement: Run output through a deterministic filter (regex for secrets, schema check, content classifier) before returning to the user.
rule.rationale: The LLM cannot be trusted to redact its own output; mechanical post-filters are the only defence in depth.

rule.statement: Never `eval`, `exec`, shell-out, or fetch-by-URL on raw LLM output without an allowlist gate.
rule.rationale: Direct execution of model output is RCE-by-design; allowlist gates and explicit tool schemas are the only correct primitive.

rule.statement: Run a red-team suite (injection probes, jailbreak prompts, exfiltration attempts) before shipping; treat every new prompt as a new attack surface.
rule.rationale: Red-team suites catch the bugs prompts engineers do not think about; without them, the first attacker is the customer.
